Title: Ecuador Constitutional Court Case: mining in the Nangaritza River
Theoretical Framing: "In summary, it states: 'The trial judge ruled the case without considering the arguments and evidence provided by the legitimate asset to argue the lack of obligation of the State to protect, respect and guarantee constitutional rights, specifically the rights of nature" (Page 8 of Second Instance).
Initiating Actor Name: Darwin Andrés Riera Duchitanga (Water Defenders’ Network)
Synopsis: Ecuador's Constitutional Court admitted a lawsuit on alleged rights of nature violations by granting mining titles in a protected area of the Nangaritza River.

Title: Ecuador Constitutional Court Case: Cuembí Triangle Protective Forest
Theoretical Framing: "This Court recognizes the importance of the declarations of protective forests, these zones being destined to the 'conservation of water, soil, flora and fauna'. These declarations are in harmony with the constitutional provisions relating to the rights of nature, to the protection of the environment and guarantee the right of the population to live in a healthy and ecologically balanced environment" (Page 37).
Initiating Actor Name: Local and Indigenous communities in the Sucumbíos Province
Synopsis: In 2021, Ecuador's Constitutional Court declared a Ministerial agreement on the Cuembí Triangle unconstitutional for violating the rights of Indigenous peoples.

Title: Ecuador court case: prior consultation in hydrocarbon areas
Theoretical Framing: "The Constitutional Sentence that has not been complied with is 001-10-SIN-CC of the Constitutional Court, in the case of the public action of unconstitutionality of the Mining Law." (Page 1 of demand)
Initiating Actor Name: Confederation of Indigenous Nationalities of Ecuador
Synopsis: In 2013, a court ruled the National Assembly didn’t comply with a sentence from the constitutional court concerning the illegality of a mining law in Ecuador.

Title: Ecuador Constitutional Court Case: municipal popular consultation on mining activities in Cuenca Canton
Theoretical Framing: "From the reading of these recitals, this Court identifies that presenting this type of absolute assertions, such as that rights cannot have material sustainability unless conditions related to the rights of nature are protected..." (Page 6).
Initiating Actor Name: Municipal Council of Cuenca
Synopsis: The court approved Cuenca Municipal Council and the Decentralized Autonomous Government of Cuenca canton’s 5 questions on mining activities in Cuenca.

Title: Ecuador court case: breach of Constituent Mandate No. 6
Theoretical Framing: "Since mining is an activity of manipulation of nature, non-compliance with articles 1 and 3 of Constituent Mandate No. 6, allows companies to carry out their activities without the due protection of the environment, required by law and corrected by the mandate, violating the rights of nature" (Page 9).
Initiating Actor Name: Federation of Peasant and Indigenous Organizations of Azuay FOA- Community Water Systems of Azuay UNAGUA
Synopsis: The court denied an action presenting a breach of articles 1 and 3 of Constituent Mandate No. 6 (rules about mining activity) violating the rights of Nature.

Title: Ecuador court case: Food Sovereignty Law Article. 16
Theoretical Framing: "It emphasizes that it is important to consider that with the issuance of the 2008 Constitution, when speaking of the rights of the nature, it does so as a complement to the rights and guarantees granted to the people. It exposes that the 'uncontested norm speaks of the production of fisheries and aquaculture, the opposite demand over the manglar, as well as the expiration of the system, and it possesses contradictory rights to them' " (Page 9 of opinion).
Initiating Actor Name: Leader Góngora Farías Fausto as common attorney of 120 people
Synopsis: The constitutional court denied an action claiming subsections in the Organic Law of the Food Sovereignty Regime violate human rights and the rights of nature.

Title: Ecuador court case: illegal mining activity in La Cero
Theoretical Framing: "...because it is thanks to nature that the life of citizens develops, who must live in harmony with it; consideration that is made, so that the rights of nature are also protected in this judgment" (Page 6).
Initiating Actor Name: Provincial Court of Justice, Canton Pastaza
Synopsis: In 2012, an Ecuador court partially approved rights of nature violations regarding mining activity in La Cero.

Title: Ecuador Constitutional Court Case: mangroves and shrimp farms in the El Verdum Commune
Theoretical Framing: "From the examination of the expository, considering and decisive part of the contested sentence, it is determined that the members of the El Verdum commune (legitimized active in the protection action and third parties with an interest in this action), raised the protection for an imminent forced displacement of the ancestral territory, destruction of mangroves, felling of trees, where -they say- they have been developing the activities of collecting shells, crabs, artisanal fishing, which serves as a livelihood for 70 families. The claim of said guardianship extends to the judge ordering the reparation that consists of the restoration of the ecosystem and the rights of nature, whose satisfaction is imputed to Mr. Jefferson Antonio Loor Moreira, now legitimated active in this action" (Page 12).
Initiating Actor Name: El Verdum Commune
Synopsis: In 2012, Ecuadorian courts ruled that a shrimp company in the mangrove ecosystem of the El Verdum Commune does not violate the rights of nature

Title: Ecuador Constitutional Court Case: chevron oil spill in the Shushufindi and Aguarico Rivers
Theoretical Framing: "Como se observa, los daños irrogados no siempre son materiales, ya que en este caso, nos encontramos ante un evidente daño inmaterial, a lo que algunos doctrinarios califican como daños extrapatrimoniales o daños morales, y se trata de una minoración en la tranquilad anímica y espiritual de fa comunidad, equivalente a una lesión a intereses colectivos no patrimoniales. Los daños expatrimoniales se caracterizan por que en ellos puede existir un matiz social, en la medida que surgen de las relaciones de la persona con el ambiente donde se desarrollan. Esta naturaleza de daño es precisamente la que el juez de primera instancia ha pretendido reparar en el caso en concreto, pues como se observa de la sentencia dictada por el pjesidente de ia Corit Provincial de Justicia de Sucurnbíos se ha determinado que los daños ambientales causados por Chevron afectan al ecosistema donde viven grupos humanos cuya integridad cultural está firmemente asociada con la salud del territorio, a lo que el juez a catalogado como impacto cultural" (Page 127)
Initiating Actor Name: Constitutional Court
Synopsis: In 2018, the Constitutional Court of Ecuador established that the Chevron oil spill damaged dependency relationships between indigenous communities and nature.

Title: Ecuador court case: mangrove felling in the Bajo Alto estuary
Theoretical Framing: "In addition, the Ministry of the Branch has already determined the administrative responsibility of the person triggered by the undue and indiscriminate felling of mangroves in the area subject to this Protection Action, facts that have been carried out repeatedly, which leads to a violation of the rights of nature guaranteed in the Constitution of the Republic of Ecuador" (Page 10).
Initiating Actor Name: Jorge Alberto Jaramillo Sánchez
Synopsis: A municipal court in El Guabo ruled a man violated the rights of Nature for felling a Mangrove to create a shrimp farm in 2020.

Title: Ecuador court case: mining in Piñas Canton
Theoretical Framing: "...declares the violation of the consitutional right of nature to the comprehensive respect for their existence, the maintenance and regeneration of their life cycles, and the right of the communities "La Mesa" and "El Pórtete," and of the people to the goodlive, water and a healthy environment..." (Page 16 of first judgement).
Initiating Actor Name: Liliana Maribel Ramírez Sánchez, representative of the group "nature and future generations;" Rosa Magaly Aguilar Ayala, representative of the Pórtete community; and Manuel Jesús Quizhpilema Paguay representative of the subject of the rights of nature Cerro "La Chuya" and the environmental group Yasunidos Piñas.
Synopsis: The court approved declared mining activities violated the rights of nature along with the rights of the communities "La Mesa" and "El Pórtete."

Title: Ecuador Constitutional Court Case: rights of the Dulcepamba River
Theoretical Framing: "I know Has made to nature already the community in the reports issued by all these entities istatales from the year 2015, the company hydroelectric HIDROTAMBO has not complied with what I know it has requested and the state entities they have not given the correct follow-up, incurring on a omission by the State that would result in the violation of the rights of nature and of the people who they live in the community of San Pablo de Amali" (Page 1 of Constitutional Court Selection).
Initiating Actor Name: Community of San Pablo de Amalí; Ombudsman's Office; Ecumenical Human Rights Commission (CEDHU)
Synopsis: In 2019, a case on rights of nature violations to the Dulcepamba river by the San José de Tambo hydroelectric company was selected by the Constitutional court.

Title: Ecuador Constitutional Court Case: Tigre River treatment plant in Mera Canton
Theoretical Framing:
"Se advierte que la Sala Única de la Corte Provincial de Justicia de Pastaza no efectuó la correspondiente verificación de si existió o no vulneración de los derechos
constitucionales expuestos en la decisión impugnada con relación a los hechos fácticos y la normativa jurídica aplicable al caso concreto, debido a que la sentencia se limitó, únicamente, a transcribir textualmente los supuestos derechos constitucionales infringidos, entre ellos, los derechos relacionados con el medio ambiente sano y con la naturaleza, pero no efectuó, conforme era exigible, un ejercicio razonable e inteligible que tuviese como finalidad comprobar la forma en que una resolución administrativa que sancionó al Gobierno Autónomo Descentralizado Municipal del cantón Mera por actuar sin el permiso ambiental respectivo, vulneró dichos derechos constitucionales" (Page 14 Constitutional Court Judgement)
Initiating Actor Name: David Ricardo Salvador Peña (Director of the Ministry of Environment in Pastaza Province)
Synopsis: In 2015, the Constitutional Court of Ecuador admitted an appeal for protection regarding the expansion of a drinking water project.

Title: Ecuador Constitutional Court Case: Escazú Agreement
Theoretical Framing: "La Constitución de la República reconoce en su artículo 71 a la naturaleza como un sujeto de derechos, y garantiza a toda persona, comunidad, pueblo o nacionalidad la posibilidad de exigir a la autoridad pública el cumplimiento de sus derechos. En ese sentido, establece la obligación del Estado de incentivar a las personas naturales y jurídicas, y a los colectivos, para que protejan la naturaleza, y de promoverel respeto a todos los elementosque forman un ecosistema." (Page 3).
Initiating Actor Name: Presidency of the Republic of Ecuador
Synopsis: The Constitutional Court of Ecuador decided the Ezcasu Agreement is consistent with constitutional protections for nature and doesn’t need legislative approval.

Title: Ecuador Administrative Sanction: Secoya Nation (cutting native forest to plant palm)
Theoretical Framing: Rights of nature
Initiating Actor Name: Ecuadorian Ministry of Environment
Synopsis: Ecuador’s Ministry of Environment fined Secoya group for cutting native forest, citing this as a violation of rights of nature.

Title: Ecuador court case: oil exploitation in Yasuní National Park
Theoretical Framing: "CUARTA.- La práctica de actividades industriales, extractivas, agrícolas, realizadas sin preocupación por los efectos que sobre el ambiente y las personas podrían ocasionar, que han determinado fenómenos alarmantes como el calentamiento global, la erosión de la capa de ozono, el peligro de agotamiento de recursos renovables y no renovables, la desertificación de la tierra, entre otros y la consecuente afectación al ambiente y la vida de los seres vivos y fundamentalmente de los seres humanos, determinó que la comunidad internacional, a través de diversos instrumentos, adoptara principios de política de protección del medio ambiente que los Estados y la sociedad deberán observar para proteger el ambiente, el que constituye un derecho supranacional, en tanto los factores ambientales sobrepasan las fronteras de los estados, comprometiendo la cooperación internacional" (Page 3).
Initiating Actor Name: Xavier Guillermo Bustamante Barriga; Silvana del Rocío - Rivadeneira Arcos; Rossana Justina Manosalvas Nicolalde; Sandra Ximena Loor Vela; Jorge Alban Gómez and Edgar Efraín Isch López
Synopsis: The Third Chamber of the Constitutional Court decided to suspend PETROBRAS' environmental license for an oil exploitation project in Yasuni National Park.

Title: Brazil Ecocide Law 2019
Theoretical Framing: "Art. 1 This Law amends Law No. 9.605, of 12th of February 1998, to typify the crime of ecocide and the criminal conduct of the person responsible for the disaster related to dam break" (Page 2).
Initiating Actor Name: Federal Deputy Zé Silva
Synopsis: A Brazilian ecocide law submitted by the Federal Deputy Zé Silva and approved by Brazil’s Chamber of Deputies, but not by the Federal Senate.

Title: New Zealand Policy: Te Mana o te Wai (National Policy Statement for Freshwater Management)
Theoretical Framing: "Te Mana o te Wai is a concept that refers to the fundamental importance of water and recognises that protecting the health of freshwater protects the health and well-being of the wider environment. It protects the mauri of the wai. Te Mana o te Wai is about restoring and preserving the  balance between the water, the wider environment,  and the community" (Page 5).
Initiating Actor Name: New Zealand government, including indigenous members
Synopsis: In 2020, the New Zealand government adopted the National Policy Statement for Freshwater Management to provide updated guidelines about managing freshwater.

Title: New Zealand Policy: Te Mana o te Taiao (Biodiversity Strategy)
Theoretical Framing: "The vision we set out for this strategy is not only for the return of health to the natural world in a way that we can measure but also for the return of a health and vibrancy that we can feel, touch, smell and hear, as well as an emotional reconnection with nature" (Page 10).
Initiating Actor Name: New Zealand government, including indigenous members
Synopsis: In 2020, the New Zealand government made a strategic framework for protecting, restoring, and sustainably using biodiversity—especially indigenous biodiversity.

Title: New Zealand Law: Ngāti Rangi Claims Settlement Act 2019 (rights of the Whangaehu River)
Theoretical Framing: "Te Waiū-o-Te-Ika is a living and indivisible whole from Te Wai ā-moe to the sea, comprising physical (including mineral) and metaphysical elements, giving life and healing to its surroundings and communities" (Page 48).
Initiating Actor Name: Ngāti Rangi, New Zealand government
Synopsis: In 2019, the New Zealand government passed the Ngāti Rangi Claims Settlement Act 2019, which uses an indigenous model and establishes environmental protections.

Title: Blue Mountains (Australia) City Council Declaration: rights of nature
Theoretical Framing: "On 29 April 2021 Blue Mountains City Council announced its intention to become the first Council and government entity in Australia to integrate Rights of Nature (RON) principles into its operations and practices" (Page 1).
Initiating Actor Name: Australian Earth Law Alliance; Blue Mountains City Council
Synopsis: In 2021, the Blue Mountains City Council became the first council in Australia to incorporate rights of nature principles into its operations and practices.

Title: African Commission on Human and Peoples’ Rights Resolution: sacred natural sites and territories
Theoretical Framing: "Calls on States Parties to recognise sacred natural sites and territories, and their customary governance systems, as contributing to the protection of human and peoples’ rights"
Initiating Actor Name: The Gaia Foundation; African Biodiversity Network; African Commission on Human and Peoples’ Rights
Synopsis: The Gaia Foundation and the African Biodiversity Network made a resolution recognizing sacred sites, territories and their customary governance systems in 2017.

Title: Ecuador Constitutional Court Case: 2014 Regulation of Mining Activities
Theoretical Framing: "The plaintiffs maintain that articles 86 and 136 of the contested agreement violate the rights of nature (arts. 71 and 72 of the Constitution), for requiring only administrative authorization from the Single Water Authority for the modification or diversion of water courses. Specifically, they allege that a significant deviation of the course of a river can imply irreversible damages to the seasonal rhythms and to the hydrological regime of the same" (Page 3).

Title: Ecuador Constitutional Court Case: Chibunga Urban Park Ordinance of 1997
Theoretical Framing: "Esperanza Martínez emphasizes that the Chibunga River must be recognized as a person, victim and subject of reparation because it has rights" (Page 4).
Initiating Actor Name: Esperanza Martínez Yánez and Marco Alejandro Ruiz Salgado
Synopsis: The court rejected a public action of unconstitutionality against an ordinance allowing agriculture, forestry and livestock activities the Chibunga Urban Park.

Title: Colombia court case: rights of the Fortalecillas river
Theoretical Framing: "Therefore, full recognition will be given to the Fortalecillas River, as an entity subject to rights, whose protection, conservation, maintenance and restoration will be in charge of the State and the community" (Page 27).
Initiating Actor Name: Johann Alexander Vargas Bedoya (Deputy of the Department of Huila)
Synopsis: A court in Colombia recognized the Fortalecillas River as an entity subject to rights to protection, conservation, maintenance and restoration.

Title: Ecuador Constitutional Court Case: Environmental Code provision regarding mangroves
Theoretical Framing: "Recognize that mangrove ecosystems are holders of recognized rights to nature and have the right to 'that their existence and the maintenance and regeneration of their vital cycles, structure, functions and evolutionary processes are fully respected'" (Page 40).
Initiating Actor Name: Ecuadorian Coordinator of Organizations for the Defense of Nature and Environment; the Asociación Animalista LiberaEcuador; Acción Ecológica
Synopsis: In 2021, Ecuador's Constitutional Court declared partial unconstitutionality of the Organic Environmental Code due to mangroves and rights of nature.

Title: International Rights of Nature Tribunal, 5th International Hearing: Glasgow 2021
Theoretical Framing: "...the International Tribunal for the Rights of Nature, founded upon existing normative frameworks of universal law, which are inviolable, non-negotiable and applicable to all living beings, and applying the Universal Declaration for the Rights of Mother Earth, has found that the practices set out violate a number of the rights of Mother Earth and members of the Earth Community of some of the living beings of which she is composed." (Page 2)
Initiating Actor Name: International Tribunal for the Rights of Nature; Global Alliance for the Rights of Nature (GARN)
Synopsis: Judges at the 5th International Rights of Nature Tribunal in Glasgow, Scotland heard cases and applied the Universal Declaration for the Rights of Nature.

Title: Peru court case: rights of the Marañón River
Theoretical Framing: "Recognition of the Marañón River and its tributaries as subjects of rights by the court, with intrinsic value, which must be protected, especially in attention to the spiritual value that this river has for indigenous peoples in general, and especially for the Kukama indigenous people" (page 4). "On the other hand, we propose an interpretation of the river as a subject of law, based on the fundamental right to live in a balanced environment suitable for life and based on the rights of indigenous peoples" (Page 8).
Initiating Actor Name: Huaynakana Kamatahuara Kana; Mari Luz Canaquiri Murayari
Synopsis: In March 2024, the Federation of Kukama-Kukamiria Women from Peru's Amazon won a lawsuit seeking rights for the Marañón River, due to systematic oil spills.

Title: Mexico Supreme Court Case: mangroves in Laguna del Carpintero
Theoretical Framing: "The recognition of this human right [to a healthy environment] requires understanding that humans coexist with and are part of the ecosystems that nature itself creates" (39) ... "Hence, the scope of protection of this human right seeks to regulate human activities to protect nature, which implies that its essential core of protection goes even beyond the most immediate objectives of human beings; in other words, this right not only addresses the right of human beings to live in a healthy and dignified environment, but also protects nature for the value it has in itself" (page 40).
Initiating Actor Name: Supreme Court of Justice
Ecological Actor Name: Mangrove swamp located in the Laguna del Carpenter
Synopsis: In 2018, Mexico's Supreme Court ruled an urban park violates the human right to a healthy environment, which encompasses inherent eco-centric protections.

Title: France Citizen Declaration: rights of the Tet River
Theoretical Framing: "The Tet river is a living and indivisible entity from its source to its mouth, delimited by its watershed, and has legal personality" (Page 3).
Initiating Actor Name: En Commun 66
Synopsis: In 2021, the NGO En Commun 66 in France wrote a Declaration of the Rights of the Tet River to give the river a legal personality.

Title: Ridgway (USA) Resolution: rights of the Uncompahgre River
Theoretical Framing: "The Uncompahgre River, its tributaries, and the Uncompahgre River Watershed, along with the living and other things existing naturally within them, exist and function as an integrated and interdependent system of natural communities and are therefore understood, respected, and recognized in this Resolution as a living entity, possessing fundamental and inalienable rights" (Page 3).
Initiating Actor Name: Town Council
Synopsis: In 2021, the town of Ridgway, Colorado adopted a resolution recognizing the rights of the Uncompahgre River, tributaries, and watershed.

Title: Ecuador Constitutional Court Case: rights of the Aquepi River
Theoretical Framing: "Recognize that the Aquepi River is subject and holder of the rights recognized to thenature and has the right to have its structure and operation respectedaffect its flow" (Page 24).
Initiating Actor Name: Residents of Julio Moreno Espinosa and San Vicente de Aquepi
Synopsis: The court declared that the Ministry of Water violated the rights of the Aquepi River by taking water in a quantity exceeding its ecological flow in dry season.

Title: Sauk-Suiattle (USA) Tribal Court Case: Tsuladxw (salmon) v. City of Seattle
Theoretical Framing: "Recognition that Tsuladxw (salmon) possess the 'inherent right to exist, flourish, regenerate, evolve, as well as an inherent right to restoration, recovery, and preservation' (Page 2 of press release).
Initiating Actor Name: Sauk-Suiattle Indian Tribe, in its capacity as a tribal nation and as the Sahkuméhu for and on behalf of Tsuladxw (salmon)
Synopsis: January 2022, the Sauk-Suiattle Indian Tribe sued the City of Seattle in Tribal Court seeking recognition of the rights of Tsuladxw (salmon in Tribe’s language)

Title: Panama National Rights of Nature Law of 2022
Theoretical Framing: "The law requires the state and all persons, whether natural or legal (such as corporations), to respect and protect Nature's rights. Some of Nature's rights recognized in the law include Nature's rights to exist, persist, and regenerate its life cycles; Nature's right to timely and effective restoration; and Nature's right to the preservation of its water cycles" (Press Release).
Initiating Actor Name: Callie Veelenturf (The Leatherback Project); Congressman Juan Diego Vásquez Gutiérrez
Synopsis: In 2022, Panama passed a national Rights of Nature Law that recognizes Nature as a subject of rights, its intrinsic value, and ecocentric principles.

Title: Chile Constitutional Amendment: rights of nature
Theoretical Framing: Nature has the right to be respected and to exist, regenerate, and maintain and restore its functions and dynamic balances, which include natural cycles, ecosystems and biodiversity.
Initiating Actor Name: Defensa Ambiental, Earth Law Center
Synopsis: In September 2022, Chilean voters rejected a proposed new Constitution that, among other things, recognized rights of Nature.

Title: New Zealand Law: Ngāi Tahu Claims Settlement Act 1998
Theoretical Framing: Ecosystems as living spiritual beings; Ecosystems as living spiritual beings; S 206 "The Crown acknowledges the statements made by Te Rūnanga o Ngāi Tahu of the particular cultural, spiritual, historic, and traditional association of Ngāi Tahu with the statutory areas, the texts of which are set out in Schedules 14 to 77." From 14 - 77 the Act contains the following quote relating to each area - "... represents the essence that binds the physical and spiritual elements of all things together, generating and upholding all life. All elements of the natural environment possess a life force, and all forms of life are related."

Ecological Actor Name: "statutory areas" Include nearly 100 different Ecological entities including Aoraki/Mount Cook
Synopsis: This Act required the settlement of all of Ngāi Tahu’s historical claims and contains the recognition of the mauri (life force) of each different site.

Title: New Zealand Law: Te Uri o Hau Claims Settlement Act 2002
Theoretical Framing: Areas of interest as sacred "Wahi tapu" sites and are living spiritual beings with a "mauri" life force; See statement of values by Te Uri o Hau at the end paragraph of each schedule.

Synopsis: Te Uri o Hau Settlement Act recognizes the "Mauri" life force of different areas within Te Uri o Hau's area of interest.

Title: New Zealand Law: Ngāti Tūwharetoa (Bay of Plenty) Claims Settlement Act 2005
Location: Bay of Plenty Region, Aotearoa (New Zealand)

Synopsis: Ngāti Tuwharetoa Claims Settlement Act 2005 provides commercial, financial and cultural redress and a Crown Apology to Ngāti Tuwharetoa (Bay of Plenty).

Title: New Zealand Law: Te Arawa Lakes Settlement Act 2006
Theoretical Framing: ecosystems as sacred natural sites; Premable ss's (2) and (12) Te Arawa Lakes are a spiritual and sacred natural site. The mana and wairua of Te Arawa lakes is recognised by Te Arawa and consequently the Crown -

Title: New Zealand Law: Waikato-Tainui Raupatu Claims (Waikato River) Settlement Act 2010
Theoretical Framing:
ecosystems as living spiritual beings; Preamble (1) 
"To Waikato-Tainui, the Waikato River is a tupuna (ancestor) which has mana (prestige) and in turn represents the mana and mauri (life force) of the tribe.
Respect for te mana o te awa (the spiritual authority, protective power and prestige of the Waikato River) is at the heart of the relationship between the tribe
and their ancestral river."

Title: New Zealand Law: Ngāti Manawa Claims Settlement Act 2012
Theoretical Framing: Ecosystems/rivers are sacred ancestors with their own mauri (life force); see acknowledgments s(14)(a) and (b); and (90)

Synopsis: Ngāti Manawa Claims Settlement Act 2012 acknowledges Ngāti Manawa's connection to the Rangitaiki and Wheao Rivers and the Crown must consider this in decisions.

Title: New Zealand Law: Ngāti Whare Claims Settlement Act 2012
Theoretical Framing:
"Kaitiaki (guardians) of the park"; at (20)
and s 108(2) "The purpose of the Forum is the protection and enhancement of the environmental, cultural, and spiritual health and wellbeing of the Rangitaiki River and its resources for the benefit of present and future generations."
Initiating Actor Name: The Minister for Treaty of Waitangi Negotiations, the Honourable Christopher Finlayson; and James Carlson, David Bronco Carson, Kōhiti Kōhiti, Lena Brew, Pene Olsen, Robert Taylor, and Roberta Rickard.

Theoretical Framing: Local rivers to Ngāti Pāhauwera are regarded as taonga (treasure) and the mauri (life force) of their spiritual and material well-being; See acknowledgements (12)(a)

Title: New Zealand Law: Ngāti Tūwharetoa Claims Settlement Act 2018

Synopsis: The Act provides a mechanism for Ngāti Tūwharetoa to exercise mana and kaitiakitanga over the Taupo catchment in partnership with local authorities.

Title: New Zealand Agreement: Pare Hauraki Collective Redress Deed 2018
Theoretical Framing:
All things have their own mauri (life force) and personality, requiring respect and protection';  4.2 of deed. 
Also look at 9.1 - Conservation framework - regarding kaitiakitanga

Title: New Zealand Law: Nga Wai o Maniapoto (Waipā River) Act 2012
Theoretical Framing:
ecosystems as sacred natural sites;  Preamble - section 8 
"The Waipa River is of deep, cultural significance to Maniapoto. It is a taonga to Maniapoto and respect for it lies at the heart of their spiritual and physical
wellbeing and their tribal identity and culture."
Initiating Actor Name: Minister in Charge of Treaty of Waitangi Negotiations, the Right Honourable Sir Douglas Arthur Montrose Graham, for the Crown, and the trustees of the Pouakani Claims Trust as the mandated representative of the Pouakani people
Synopsis: The Act recognizes the restoration and maintenance of the Waipa River must be consistent with the desire of Maniapoto to keep intact the mauri of the river.

Title: New Zealand Law: Taumata Arowai–the Water Services Regulator Act 2020
Theoretical Framing: "Te mana o te wai" recognises water is connected to the wider environment and community and has a "life force" in need of protection; see section 10
Initiating Actor Name: The Crown
Synopsis: One of the objectives of the act is to give effect to Te Mana o te Wai, which recognizes the importance of the health of freshwater to the wider environment.

Title: New Zealand Law: Water Services Act 2021
Theoretical Framing: "Te mana o te wai" recognises water is connected to the wider environment and community and has a "life force" in need of protection; see section 14
Initiating Actor Name: The Crown
Synopsis: The Water Services Act 2021 is what gives Taumata Arowai the legal authority to carry out its duties as New Zealand’s dedicated water regulator.

Title: New Zealand Law: Resource Management Act 1991
Theoretical Framing: "Kaitiakitanga" (guardianship" is a matter of national important;  see section 7(a)
Initiating Actor Name: The Crown
Synopsis: The Resource Management Act 1991 (RMA) is New Zealand’s current legislature governing how people interact with natural resources.

Title: New Zealand Law: Natural and Built Environments Act
Theoretical Framing: “Te Oranga o te Taiao” which incorporates the health of the natural environment and its capacity to sustain life, the relationship between iwi and hapū and te taiao (environment), and the interconnectedness of all parts of the natural environment; see Acts purpose under section 5.
Initiating Actor Name: The Crown; Honourable David Parker
Synopsis: The draft Act is premised on the principle of Te Oranga o te Taiao and is intended to be the primary piece of legislation to replace the RMA.

Title: New Zealand Policy: National Policy Statement for Indigenous Biodiversity 2019
Theoretical Framing: We are "kaitiaki" (guardians) of indigenous biodiversity which we are connected to and dependant on; see 1.7(1) fundamental concepts.
Initiating Actor Name: Biodiversity Collaborative Group, Ministry for the Environment, the New Zealand Government, all members of the public who have made submissions on the draft
Synopsis: This policy statement sets out objectives, policies and implementation requirements to manage natural resources to maintain indigenous biological diversity.

Title: New Zealand Environment Court Case: Sustainable Matatā v. Bay of Plenty Regional Council
Theoretical Framing:
Water bodies have a "mauri" life force and are connected to "tangata whenua" the people of the land who act as their "kaitiaki" guardians. Te Mana o te Wai is inextricably linked to the mana of Tangata Whenua and the mauri of the waterbody; see paragraph [398].
Te Mana o Te Wai means means something more than mauri. It encapsulates the entire water body, including the banks and beds. There is a relational aspect associated to Te Mana o Te Wai as it connects and relates to the identity of Tangata Whenua who act as their kaitiaki. - See paragraph [402].
Initiating Actor Name: Whakatane District Council, Bay of Plenty Regional Council, Sustainable Matata Inc, Papakainga Komiti Inc.
Ecological Actor Name: " Lot 6A" Wastewater Treatment Plant (“the Treatment Plant”) on land east of Matata, and a land application field (“the LAF”) to be sited on a council reserve on a dune formation.
Synopsis: The Court found that Te Mana o Te Wai means more than just the mauri of a waterbody and encapsulates the entire waterbody including its banks and beds.

Title: New Zealand Environment Court Case: Aratiatia Livestock Ltd v. Southland Regional Council
Theoretical Framing: Upholding "Te Mana o Te Wai" acknowledges and protects the "mauri" of water, which in turn sustains the hauora (health) of the environment, the waterbody, and of people; at para [17].
Initiating Actor Name: Whakatane District Council, Bay of Plenty Regional Council, Sustainable Matata Inc, Papakainga Komiti Inc.
Ecological Actor Name: " Lot 6A" Wastewater Treatment Plant (“the Treatment Plant”) on land east of Matata, and a land application field (“the LAF”) to be sited on a council reserve on a dune formation.
Synopsis: The Court declared Te Mana o Te Wai was a matter of national significance in the NPS-FM and freshwater must be managed via a framework that considers this.

Title: New Zealand court case: Ngati Rangi Trust v. Manawatu-Wanganui Regional Council
Location: Central North Island, Tongariro area, Aotearoa (New Zealand)
Theoretical Framing: The river is spiritually connected to its people; see para [55]
Initiating Actor Name: Ngati Rangi Trust
Synopsis: The decision recognises that the effect of the diversion on Tikanga Maori has both a spiritual and physical dimension.

Title: Canada Law: rights and legal personality of the St. Lawrence River
Theoretical Framing: "...to give the River the capacity of a natural person in order to protect its right to exist and flow, to maintain its vital cycles, natural biodiversity and integrity, to fulfil essential functions within its ecosystem, to be nourished by its aquifers and tributaries, to be protected from contamination and to regenerate; and to establish the St. Lawrence River Protection Committee, responsible for acting on behalf of the River" (Page 2).
Initiating Actor Name: International Observatory of the Rights of Nature (NGO); Canadian MP Alexandre Boulerice (New Democratic Party)
Synopsis: In May 2022, a bill to give legal capacity and establish the rights of the St. Lawrence River was submitted to the Canadian Parliament.

Title: Donegal County (Ireland) Motion: rights of nature
Theoretical Framing: "DCC will over the next eighteen months, collaborate with civic society including The Gathering, Friends of the Earth(NI), Trade Unions and those who have carried out biodiversity audits and drawn up biodiversity action plans for areas of Donegal to explore what Rights of Nature means for the people and economies of the region and to investigate how Rights of Nature could be expressed in community plans, corporate plans, improvement objectives and other strategic frameworks" (Page 1).
Initiating Actor Name: Albert Doherty
Synopsis: In 2021, Donegal County Council passed a motion to explore rights of nature to incorporate it into its decisions and objectives.

Title: Serro (Brazil) Municipal Organic Law: rights of nature
Theoretical Framing: "recognizes to Nature the ownership of rights to exist, prosper, and evolve" (62)
Initiating Actor Name: Councilwoman Karine Roza de Oliveira Santos; Mapas (NGO)
Synopsis: In 2022, the Municipality of Serro adopted an amendment to the Organic Law that recognizes the rights of Nature to exist, prosper, and evolve.

Title: Kerala (India) High Court Case: legal personhood for the Bharathapuzha River
Theoretical Framing: "Declare that the Bharathapuzha and its tributaries are legal entities and they have a right to flow and live without being exploited and illegal activities committed upon by the State or its agencies or by any other person" (26 of pdf; 19 of document)
Initiating Actor Name: Friends of Bharatapuzha (FoB)
Synopsis: In 2022, a petition was filed in the Kerala High Court of India seeking to declare the Bharathapuzha river as a 'legal entity' with a right to flow and live

Title: Madras (India) High Court Case: legal personhood for Mother Nature
Theoretical Framing: '“Mother Nature” as a “Living Being”...having the status of a legal person, with all corresponding rights, duties and liabilities of a living person' (21).
Initiating Actor Name: High Court of Madras
Synopsis: In 2022, the Madras High Court declared “Mother Nature” a living being, ruling it has the same legal status and rights as a human being

Title: Yucatán (Mexico) Court Case: rights of the Ring of Cenotes
Theoretical Framing: "to demand the protection and recognition of the cenotes as as subject of law, in order to protect, with the highest standards, their right to a healthy environment, their self-determination and the protection of their cultural legacy."
Initiating Actor Name: Kanan Ts'ono'ot Collective (Guardians of the Cenotes); Lourdes Medina Carillo (the collective’s lawyer_
Synopsis: In 2023, the Mayan people of Homún in Yucatán, Mexico filed a lawsuit in federal court seeking recognition of the Ring of Cenotes as subjects of law with rights

Title: White Earth Band of Ojibwe Tribal Court Case: Manoomin et al. v. Minnesota DNR
Theoretical Framing: "Plaintiffs Manoomin [and] the White Earth Band of Ojibwe...seek declaratory and injunctive relief to: a. Declare Manoomin, or wild rice...possesses inherent rights to exist, flourish, regenerate, and evolve" (11).
Initiating Actor Name: White Earth Band of Ojibwe
Synopsis: Manoomin (wild rice) sued Minnesota Department of Natural Resources in Tribal Court of the White Earth Band for violating its rights to exist and flourish

Title: Martuwarra Fitzroy River Declaration (Australia): a living ancestral being
Theoretical Framing: Under First Law of Indigenous nations of Western Australia, the Martuwarra-Fitzroy river is a sacred living ancestral being and has a right to life.
Initiating Actor Name: Traditional Owners from the Fitzroy River catchment area
Synopsis: the Fitzroy River Declaration expresses First Law of Martuwarra Nations, recognizing "the Fitrzoy River is a living ancestral being and has a right to life"

Title: Martuwarra Fitzroy River Council Conservation and Management Plan
Theoretical Framing: "Recognition of First Law, customary law and the guardianship and authority of Martuwarra Traditional Owner’s fiduciary duty to protect the Martuwarra’s right to flow as a living entity" (Martuwarra Management Plan 2020, 44)
Initiating Actor Name: Traditional Owner members of the Martuwarra Fitzroy River Council
Synopsis: the Martuwarra River Council created a Management Plan recognizing First Law and guardianship, to protect the river's right to flow as a living ancestral being

Title: Tohono O’odham Nation (USA) Resolution: personhood of saguaro cacti
Theoretical Framing: "Indigenous peoples have made the compelling argument that the trees, wildlife, rivers, and mountains are sacred and should be granted 'personhood' with all the legal rights associated with it; and the Tohono O'odham and sister O'odham tribes, hold the Ha:sañ in the highest regard and consider the Ha:sañ as an O'odham person" (1)
Initiating Actor Name: Tohono O'odham Legislative Council
Synopsis: Resolution from Tohono O'odham Legislative Council acknowledges legal personhood and affirms Ha:san (saguaros cacti) as an O’odham person, kin, and sacred plant

Title: IPBES Summary for Policymakers: diverse values and valuation of nature
Theoretical Framing: "Different rights-based approaches have been found to incorporate the diverse values of nature into local and national laws and constitutions (e.g., rights to a healthy environment, rights of nature, rights of Mother Earth, rights of specific entities like rivers, lakes, mountains)." (30)
Initiating Actor Name: Intergovernmental Science-Policy Platform on Biodiversity and Ecosystem Services (IPBES)
Synopsis: The 2022 IPBES report considers the rights of Nature and nature's intrinsic value as elements that should be present in environmental policymaking.

Title: Hopi Tribe (USA) Proclamation: preserve and protect Sípàapu
Location: Hopi and Tewa Villages of the Hopi Tribe Sovereign Nation (Arizona, USA)
Theoretical Framing: "we, the Hopi people, believe Water is Life, all earthly beings; human, birds, insects, reptiles, trees and plants, animal life have a right to Life and each have a unique role in taking care of Mother Earth and is not a personal commodity"
Initiating Actor Name: members of 12 independent Hopi villages [Lower Moencopi, Upper Moencopi, Hotevilla, Bacavi, Oraivi, Kykotsmovi, Shungopavy, Shipaulovi, Mishongnovi, Walpi, Sichomovi, Tewa, and Spider Mound]; NGO Black Mesa Trust
Synopsis: Hopi and Black Mesa Trust 'Proclamation to Preserve and Protect Sípàapu' recognizes the right to life of human and non-human beings

Title: United Nations World Charter for Nature 1982
Theoretical Framing: "mankind is part of nature and life depends on the uninterrupted functioning of natural systems" and "every form of life is unique, warranting respect regardless of its worth to man, and, to accord other organisms such recognition, man must be guided by a moral code" (17)
Initiating Actor Name: United Nations General Assembly
Synopsis: United Nations 1982 World Charter for Nature recognizes nature's intrinsic value and affirms mankind as being a part of nature

Title: Chile court case: legal personhood for the orangutan Sandai
Theoretical Framing: petition for habeas corpus seeking the recognition of legal personhood for a orangutan whose rights have been violated by a deprivation of freedom from being kept in captivity at a zoo
Initiating Actor Name: Fundación Justicia Interespecie
Synopsis: July 2022, a habeas corpus petition was filed in Chile to recognize the legal personhood of a 28 year old orangutan named Sandai—which was subsequently rejected

Title: Declaration for the Protection of the Amazon Sacred Headwaters
Location: Amazon Basin of Ecuador and Northern Peru
Theoretical Framing: "a bottom-up participatory process of visioning a future for the region based on the recognition and respect for indigenous peoples' collective rights, the rights of nature and the pursuit of collective wellbeing — also known as Buen Vivir o Vida Plena." (1-2)
Initiating Actor Name: Amazon Sacred Headwaters Alliance
Ecological Actor Name: The Sacred Headwaters of the Amazon: Territories for Life
Synopsis: Declaration by Indigenous federations of Ecuador and Peru to protect Amazon Sacred Headwaters and respect the rights of Nature and Buen Vivir o Vida Plena

Title: Amazon Sacred Headwaters Bioregional Plan 2030
Location: Amazon Basin of Ecuador and Northern Peru
Theoretical Framing: "recognizing this sacred nature of life helps us deepen our understanding of the close relationship between Human Rights and the Rights of Nature that this Initiative seeks to uphold in Ecuador and Peru." (14)
Initiating Actor Name: Amazon Sacred Headwaters Alliance (complete list on pg. 6-7 of the Plan)
Ecological Actor Name: the Sacred Headwaters of the Amazon: Territories for Life
Synopsis: The Amazon Sacred Headwaters Initiative recognizes the inherent rights of Nature in their Bioregional Plan 2030 for the Amazon basin of Ecuador and Peru

Title: Kawsak Sacha Proposal: protected areas category
Theoretical Framing: "Kawsak Sacha is a robust proposal capable of defending the Rights of Nature as it is enshrined in the Ecuadorian Constitution, which, in addition, also legally recognizes the importance of maintaining healthy ecosystems as a foundation for Sumak Kawsay. Regarding the Rights of Nature, our proposal emphasizes that in order to extend rights to Nature, one must first recognize its entities as persons (and not mere objects). As persons, the beings of the forest relate to one another as well as to the Indigenous People that share their land. So, based on our continuous life together with the beings of the forest, Kawsak Sacha emerges as an authentic way of guaranteeing the Rights of Nature in those spaces that have not yet been decimated." (2)
Initiating Actor Name: Kichwa People of Sarayaku
Synopsis: In 2018, the Kawsak Sacha (Living Forest) proposal to recognize a new legal category of protected area and entities of the Living Forest as persons, not objects

Title: Kawsak Sacha Declaration: The Living Forest
Theoretical Framing: "DECLARES: The territory of Sarayaku KAWSAK SACHA - LIVING FOREST, BEING ALIVE AND CONSCIOUS, and SUBJECT OF RIGHTS, as we have traditionally recognized as original peoples and nationalities." (1)
Initiating Actor Name: Kichwa People of Sarayaku
Synopsis: The Kichwa People of Sarayaku adopted the Declaration of Kawsak Sacha (Living Forest), recognizing their territory as a living being and subject of rights.

Title: IUCN World Conservation Congress Recommendation 102: Indigenous Kahuʻāina guardians of sacred lands
Theoretical Framing: "Mother Earth constitutes a holistic living being. Sacred landscapes and their natural features must be protected...to maintain the balance of all life on Mother Earth" (1)
Initiating Actor Name: Indigenous Kahuʻāina Guardians of sacred lands
Synopsis: 2016 IUCN statement of Indigenous Guardians of Sacred Lands recognizing Mother Earth as a holistic living being and affirms sacred natural sites as No-Go areas

Title: Ponca Nation (USA) Resolution: rights of rivers
Theoretical Framing: statute recognizing the "immutable Rights of Rivers” for two rivers considered sacred feminine entities for the Ponca people
Initiating Actor Name: Ponca Tribe of Indians of Oklahoma
Ecological Actor Name: Ní’skà (the Arkansas River) and Ni’ží’dè (the Salt Fork River)
Synopsis: the Ponca Nation adopted the Rights of Rivers law, a new statute recognizing the immutable legal rights of two rivers that flow through Ponca territory

Title: Hawaii (USA) State Law: Mauna Kea stewardship and oversight authority
Theoretical Framing: "The legislature finds that Mauna Kea serves as an important cultural and genealogical site to the people of Hawaii, particularly to Native Hawaiians. The summit region of Mauna Kea is a spiritual and special place of significance" (pg. 1). "The purpose of this Act is to establish a new governance and management structure for Mauna Kea that collaboratively engages with all stakeholders, particularly the Native Hawaiian community." (pg. 2). "The plan shall:...Consider the State’s energy and sustainability goals, as well as impacts to climate change...Incorporate indigenous management and cultural processes and values...acknowledge and contextualize unresolved social justice issues that underpin Mauna Kea." (pg. 15)
Initiating Actor Name: Hawaii state legislature
Synopsis: Hawaii passes Act recognizing Mauna Kea as a sacred spiritual site and establishes a stewardship authority based on Indigenous management.

Title: U’wa Nation (Colombia) Indigenous Declaration: ancestral territory and sacred site Zizuma (El Cocuy National Park)
Theoretical Framing: "Zizuma (PNNC), is for the U'wa nation our sacred territory, where our divine beings coexist, and the ancestral and cultural knowledge of our traditional authorities (Werjayas)materializes...We ratify our position of defense and protection of mother earth, respect for our sacred sites, life and survival as an ancient and ancient people...a step towards the guarantee of life, culture and Mother Earth."
Initiating Actor Name: Association of Traditional U’wa Authorities and Councils (ASOU’WA)
Synopsis: In March 2016, the U’wa people ratified their position of territorial defense of their ancestral sacred site Mt. Zizuma in Colombia’s El Cocuy National Park.

Title: Africa Protected Areas Congress (2022) Kigali Declaration: “We are Nature”
Theoretical Framing:
"Implement and or formulate policies and laws that respect Indigenous Peoples and Local Community tenure-led conservation thought and practice, embracing and upholding the relationship and interaction between people, land, and nature." (4)
"wildlife exists in Africa because African people are nature" (5)
Initiating Actor Name: Indigenous Peoples, Local Communities, and organizations of 40+ African nations
Synopsis: In 2022, representatives of Indigenous Peoples and Local Communities from more than 40 African nations made the Kigali Declaration "We Are Nature."

Title: Alto Paraíso (Brazil) Municipal Organic Law: rights of nature
Theoretical Framing: rights of nature
Initiating Actor Name: Municipality of Alto Paraíso de Goiás
Synopsis: On 17 August 2022, the Municipality of Alto Paraíso de Goiás in Brazil, amended its organic law to recognize rights of nature.

Title: UN Secretary-General Report (2022): Harmony with Nature
Theoretical Framing: "parliamentarians and politicians from Brazil, Canada, Mexico, Nigeria, Panama and Spain. In partnership with civil society organizations, they are leading efforts to recognize the rights of Nature in their respective countries and to implement ecological economics, for a future in which humankind is again living with respect for Mother Earth and in harmony with Nature." (2)
Initiating Actor Name: United Nations General Assembly
Synopsis: UN Harmony with Nature report recognizes advances in Earth jurisprudence, particularly rights of Nature, and stresses need for a sustainable regenerative world

Title: Grand Lake (USA) Town Resolution: rights of the Grand Lake watershed
Theoretical Framing: "Grand Lake, its Watershed, its life forms, and other natural components exist and function as an integrated and interdependent system of natural communities, and are therefore, recognized, understood, and respected in this Resolution as a living entity, possessing fundamental and inalienable rights" (3)
Initiating Actor Name: Grand Lake Board of Trustees and Town Attorney; Save the Colorado
Synopsis: Rights of Nature Resolution for the Grand Lake Watershed recognizes Grand Lake Watershed as a living entity that possesses fundamental and inalienable rights

Title: Benin National Sacred Forest Law of 2012
Theoretical Framing: "The sacred forest should be sustainably managed by the community to maintain its ecological, economic, socio-cultural, spiritual and recreational functions" (2)
Initiating Actor Name: GRABE-Benin
Synopsis: The Benin Sacred Forest Law passed in 2012 recognizing sacred forests where gods, spirits and ancestors reside, and communities that protect and govern them.

Title: National Congress of American Indians (USA) Resolution: supporting rights of nature
Theoretical Framing: "the National Congress of American Indians (NCAI) supports the rights of nature legal framework and the efforts of Tribal Nations to recognize and enforce these rights within tribal law and governance"
Initiating Actor Name: Menīkānaehkem; the Center for Democratic and Environmental Rights (CDER)
Synopsis: National Congress of American Indians (NCAI) resolution supporting rights of Nature and efforts of Tribal Nations to recognize and enforce within tribal law

Title: Newfoundland & Labrador (Canada) Law: rights of the Atlantic Ocean
Theoretical Framing: "Bill acknowledging the rights of the Atlantic Ocean"
Initiating Actor Name: Social Justice Co-operative of Newfoundland & Labrador (SJCNL); Climate Action Newfoundland & Labrador (CANL)
Synopsis: The Social Justice Co-operative of Newfoundland & Labrador revealed the first draft of a new "Rights of the Atlantic Ocean" bill to be brought in St. John's, NL

Title: Faith in Nature (UK) Nature Governance: nature on the board
Theoretical Framing: "By voluntarily recognising the legal Rights of Nature within our structures, Faith In Nature set out to forever reverse this injustice — seeking counsel from Nature in order to make better, more responsible decisions that take the natural world into account." (1)
Initiating Actor Name: Faith in Nature; Lawyers for Nature; Earth Law Center
Synopsis: In August 2022, Faith In Nature became the first company in the world to appoint Nature to its board of directors.

Title: Oaxaca State (Mexico) Law: rights of rivers and other water sources
Theoretical Framing: "la defensa, la tutela, y el reconocimiento del los derechos de los Rios y otros Fuentes Hidricas, considerando sus cuencas hidrologicas, y sus ecosistemas asociados" (2)
Initiating Actor Name: Fredie Delfín Avendaño (congressman); Horacio Soza Villavicencio (congressman); Carlos Morales Sanchez (Litigio Estratégico Indígena A.C.); Claudia Brindis (UN Harmony with Nature; Grant Wilson (Earth Law Center)
Synopsis: In 2020, a bill was submitted to the congress of Oaxaca (Mexico) to establish rivers and other water sources in the state of Oaxaca as subjects with rights.

Title: Mornington Peninsula (Australia) Council Resolution: exploring rights of nature frameworks
Theoretical Framing: "considering that the ‘Rights of Nature’ and biodiversity have the right to exist and flourish on the Mornington Peninsula."
Initiating Actor Name: Councillor Debra Mar
Synopsis: Mornington Peninsula Shire Council of Australia passed a resolution to explore Rights of Nature frameworks for Council strategies, plans and future actions

Title: Woodbridge (UK) Citizen Declaration: Rights of the River Deben
Theoretical Framing: "1. the right to flow freely, 2. the right to perform essential functions within its ecosystem, 3. the right to be free from pollution, 4. to feed and be fed by sustainable aquifers, 5. the right to to native biodiversity, 6. the right to regeneration and restoration"
Initiating Actor Name: Councillor Caroline Page; Ruth Leach (Save The Deben)
Synopsis: In 2022 a citizens declaration of the rights of the River Deben was presented by residents of the town of Woodbridge, England.

Title: Buliisa (Uganda) District Ordinance: Bagungu customary law and sacred sites (2020)
Theoretical Framing: "The law provides for preservation of Sacred Natural Sites (SNS) deriving from Earth Jurisprudence principles and practice, use of indigenous knowledge and practices to preserve the eco-system (customary governance systems) as well as the law on the rights of Nature." (ANARDE, 1)
Initiating Actor Name: Indigenous Bagungu People; Buliisa District Council; support from the African Institute for Culture and Ecology (AFRICE), Advocates for Natural Resources and Development (ANARDE), and GAIA Foundation.
Synopsis: In December 2020 the Buliisa District Council of Uganda passed a law recognizing the customary laws of the Bagungu People and sacred natural sites (Mpuluma)

Title: Ireland Citizens Assembly Proposal: constitutional amendment to recognize rights of nature
Theoretical Framing: Rights of nature; "towards a living island of rights-bearing communities"
Initiating Actor Name: Dr Ciara Brennan, Director, EJNI; Dr Peter Doran, QUB School of Law/EJNI; Alison Hough BL, Technological University of the Shannon/EJNI; Sabrina Joyce-Kemper, Environmental Advocate; Dr Rachel Killean, Sydney Law School; Prof Peadar Kirby, Cloughjordan Ecovillage, University of Limerick; Bróna McNeill LLM, QUB Law School/EJNI; Claire Nevin BL, Lawyers for Nature/Ecojustice Ireland, Barrister; Declan Owens LLB LLM BL, Ecojustice Ireland, Solicitor; Lynda Sullivan, Writer and Earth Activist/EJNI; Dr Brendan Tobin, Marie-Sklowdowska Curie Research Fellow at National University of Ireland, Galway
Synopsis: In November 2022 Ireland's Citizens Assembly on Biodiversity recommended the Irish Government amend the constitution to recognize rights of nature.

Title: Germany Federal Constitutional Amendment: rights of nature
Theoretical Framing: (2) The dignity of nature requires that the natural foundations of life be protected, cared for, and preserved, and that the intrinsic value of the natural world be respected in its entirety; (3) The German people therefore recognize inviolable and inalienable human and natural rights as the foundation of every human community, as well as of peace and justice in the world; (4) Fundamental rights also apply to nature insofar as they are applicable to it. Nature has legal capacity and must be respected and protected by legislation, the executive, and the judiciary.
Initiating Actor Name: German Network for the Rights of Nature
Synopsis: In April 2022, the German Network for the Rights of Nature (NGO) developed a proposal to amend the German Constitution to recognize the Rights of Nature.

Title: Bavaria (Germany) State Constitutional Amendment: Rights of Nature
Theoretical Framing: "rights of the natural environment"
Initiating Actor Name: Hans Leo Bader, German Network for the Rights of Nature
Synopsis: In September 2021, citizens in Bavaria (Germany) launched a referendum initiative to recognize rights of nature in Bavaria's constitution.

Title: Argentina court case: rights of jaguars in the Gran Chaco ecoregion
Location: Chaco, Salta, Santiago del Estero and Formosa Provinces, Argentina
Theoretical Framing: "allow said species to continue to exist, enjoy its ecosystems, maintaining its plans for life, health, well-being, food, freedom, reproduction and security of the last less than 20 vulnerable subjects" (Greenpeace) “As part of Nature, jaguars have rights. The Law has recognized rights to companies, which are abstract, fictional, intangible entities, but -on the other hand- until now it has not recognized rights to Nature and its species that are real, alive and tangible." (Greenpeace Argentina lawyer Enrique Viale)
Initiating Actor Name: Fundación Greenpeace Argentina; Argentine Association of Environmental Lawyers/CAJE
Synopsis: In 2019 Greenpeace brought a case before the Supreme Court of Justice in Argentina arguing for recognition of the rights of the entire Jaguar species

Title: Ecuador Law: Justice and Protection for the Páramos
Theoretical Framing:
"water as a fundamental right, as a service, as a source and guarantee for the preservation of life and, above all, as an imperative and non-delegable need to preserve the paramos as natural water sources." (2)
"establish guidelines that guarantee their conservation, integrity, preservation, restoration, sustainable use, generation of knowledge and limitations of dominion." (7)
Initiating Actor Name: National Assembly of Ecuador
Synopsis: In 2022 a legislator from Carchi Province submitted to Ecuador's National Assembly a bill to guarantee the protection of páramo [moorland] ecosystems

Title: Port Townsend (USA) Proclamation: rights of the southern resident orca
Theoretical Framing: “The rights of the Southern Resident Orcas include, but are not limited to, the right to life, autonomy, culture, free and safe passage, adequate food supply from naturally occurring sources, and freedom from conditions causing physical, emotional or mental harm, including a habitat degraded by noise, pollution and contamination"
Initiating Actor Name: Earth Law Center (ELC); Legal Rights for the Salish Sea (LRSS); the City Council of Port Townsend
Synopsis: Dec. 5 2022, the city council of Port Townsend, Washington made a non-binding proclamation that Southern Resident Orca whales have legal rights

Title: Gig Harbor (USA) Proclamation: rights of the southern resident orca
Theoretical Framing: “The rights of the Southern Resident Orcas include, but are not limited to, the right to life, autonomy, culture, free and safe passage, adequate food supply from naturally occurring sources, and freedom from conditions causing physical, emotional or mental harm, including a habitat degraded by noise, pollution and contamination"
Initiating Actor Name: Earth Law Center (ELC); Legal Rights for the Salish Sea (LRSS); City Council of Gig Harbor
Synopsis: Dec. 12 2022, the city council of Gig Harbor, Washington made a non-binding proclamation that Southern Resident Orca whales have legal rights

Title: Wilhelmshaven Declaration: Wadden Sea management plan
Theoretical Framing: "Instruct the Wadden Sea Board to explore if the concept of “Rights of Nature” could be adapted to our common Wadden Sea World Heritage" (3)
Initiating Actor Name: Ministers representing Denmark, Netherlands, and Germany in the Trilateral Wadden Sea Governmental Council
Synopsis: The 2022 Trilateral Wadden Sea Governmental Council management plan to explore if “Rights of Nature” could be adapted to the Wadden Sea World Heritage.

Title: North Carolina State (USA) Law: rights of the Haw River ecosystem
Theoretical Framing: "This act recognizes and protects the rights of the Haw River ecosystem and the right of the people of North Carolina to a healthy, thriving Haw River ecosystem." (1)
Initiating Actor Name: Dr. Crystal Cavalier-Keck of the Occaneechi Band of the Saponi Nation; 7 Directions of Service (NGO); Center for Democratic and Environmental Rights (CDER); Native American Caucus of the North Carolina Democratic Party; Representatives Harrison, Autry, and Morey (primary legislative sponsors)
Synopsis: In April 2023, the Rights of the Haw River Ecosystem Act was introduced in the NC state assembly to recognize the legal rights of the Haw River.

Title: Ecuador Constitutional Court Case: rights of the Monjas River
Theoretical Framing: To recognize that the Monjas River is a subject and holder of the recognized rights to nature and has the right to "full respect for its existence and the maintenance and regeneration of its vital cycles, structure, functions and evolutionary processes"; and to declare that the Municipality of the Metropolitan District of Quito violated its rights. (p. 48)
Initiating Actor Name: Ann Arlene and Pamela Lilian Monge Froebelius
Synopsis: Ecuador's Constitutional Court rules that urban expansion into a fragile watershed by Quito's municipal government violated the rights of the Monjas River.

Title: Netherlands Citizen Declaration: rights of the Maas River
Theoretical Framing: the Meuse should possess at least the following fundamental rights: 1. The right to flow 2. The right to perform the essential ecological functions in its ecosystem, including providing healthy housing for fish, amphibians, aquatic plants and other river life 3. The right to be free from pollution 4. The right to feed and be fed by a healthy underground aquifer 5. The right to native biodiversity 6. The Right to Restoration.
Initiating Actor Name: Jessica den Outer; Maas Cleanup campaign
